records.ety: [OE. pas, F. pas, from L. passus a step, pace, orig., a stretching out of the feet in walking; cf. pandere, passum, to spread, stretch; perh. akin to E. patent. Cf. Pas, Pass.]

defs: A single movement from one foot to the other in walking; a step.
The length of a step in walking or marching, reckoned from the heel of one foot to the heel of the other; -- used as a unit in measuring distances; as, he advanced fifty paces.
notes: ☞ Ordinarily the pace is estimated at two and one half linear feet; but in measuring distances be stepping, the pace is extended to three feet (one yard) or to three and three tenths feet (one fifth of a rod). The regulation marching pace in the English and United States armies is thirty inches for quick time, and thirty-six inches for double time. The Roman pace (passus) was from the heel of one foot to the heel of the same foot when it next touched the ground, five Roman feet.

defs: Manner of stepping or moving; gait; walk; as, the walk, trot, canter, gallop, and amble are paces of the horse; a swaggering pace; a quick pace.
quotes: To-morrow, and to-morrow, and to-morrow, Creeps in this petty pace from day to day.
In the military schools of riding a variety of paces are taught.

defs: Specifically, a kind of fast amble; a rack.
Any single movement, step, or procedure.
quotes: The first pace necessary for his majesty to make is to fall into confidence with Spain.

defs: A broad step or platform; any part of a floor slightly raised above the rest, as around an altar, or at the upper end of a hall.
A device in a loom, to maintain tension on the warp in pacing the web.
The rate of progress of any process or activity; as, the students ran at a rapid pace; the plants grew at a remarkable pace.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Paced (pāst); p. pr. & vb. n. Pacing (pā"sng).]

defs: To go; to walk; specifically, to move with regular or measured steps.

quotes: Or [ere] that I further in this tale pace.

defs: To move quickly by lifting the legs on the same side together, as a horse; to amble with rapidity; to rack.
To walk over with measured tread; to move slowly over or upon; as, the guard paces his round.
To measure by steps or paces; as, to pace a piece of ground. Often used with out; as, to pace out the distance.
To develop, guide, or control the pace or paces of; to teach the pace; to break in.
quotes: If you can, pace your wisdom In that good path that I would wish it go.
